mnemonics.title: File Mode Remembering
mnemonics.content:
Modes: APPend adds at end, TRUNC truncates, ATE at end (allow move), BInary no translate, IN input, OUT output.

Mnemonic: 'PTAIBIO' - Please Take A Pill Before I Order' (for file modes in order of importance).

acronyms.usage: Used for reading data from files - like cin but for files
Used for writing data to files - like cout but for files
Bidirectional - both input and output to files
Move the read pointer to a specific position
Move the write pointer to a specific position
Returns current position of read pointer
Returns current position of write pointer

visualTips.visualization: Think of a tape recorder - 'g' for get (listen/read) and 'p' for put (record/write). You position the head to read from or write to specific locations.
Imagine a file as a long ribbon. tellg/tellp tell you where your finger is on the ribbon. seekg/seekp move your finger to a specific position on the ribbon.
Text mode is like reading a book - characters are interpreted. Binary mode is like looking at the book's raw paper and ink - exactly what's there, no interpretation.
APPend = permanently stuck at end (like superglue). ATE = at end initially but can move around (like velcro).

analogies.analogy: File streams are like TV remotes. ofstream is like the 'Record' button - sending data to the TV (file). ifstream is like the 'Play/View' button - receiving data from the TV.

Opening a file is like opening a document: ios::trunc is getting a fresh paper (old one thrown away), ios::app is adding sticky notes to the end, ios::ate is flipping to the last page but can go back.
seekg(10, ios::cur) is like saying 'from where I am now, move 10 pages forward' - relative positioning just like turning pages in a book.
